research_question: What repetition ranges can produce hypertrophy?
conclusion: A broad loading spectrum can produce hypertrophy when sets are sufficiently effortful; moderate loads are usually the most time-efficient default.
practical_range_text: Approximately 5-30 reps; 6-15 is the practical default for many exercises
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: A broad loading spectrum can produce hypertrophy when sets are sufficiently effortful; moderate loads are usually the most time-efficient default.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How should effort change with load?
conclusion: As loads become lighter and rep targets rise, sets generally need to finish closer to task failure to recruit and fatigue high-threshold motor units.
practical_range_text: Heavy/moderate: often 1-4 RIR; light/high-rep: often 0-2 RIR
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: As loads become lighter and rep targets rise, sets generally need to finish closer to task failure to recruit and fatigue high-threshold motor units.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Is momentary failure required?
conclusion: Momentary muscular failure is not required for hypertrophy and can impose extra fatigue; it is more defensible selectively on stable isolation work than routinely on high-skill compounds.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Momentary muscular failure is not required for hypertrophy and can impose extra fatigue; it is more defensible selectively on stable isolation work than routinely on high-skill compounds.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How does weekly set volume affect hypertrophy?
male_population: healthy adult males eligible for resistance training
conclusion: Hypertrophy generally increases with weekly set volume, but marginal returns diminish and individual recovery constrains useful dose.
practical_range_text: Start near 6-10 fractional hard sets; many trained males use roughly 10-20, adjusted by response
key_limitations: The numeric bands are operational starting ranges, not established per-muscle thresholds.
application_rule: Hypertrophy generally increases with weekly set volume, but marginal returns diminish and individual recovery constrains useful dose.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How should indirect sets be counted?
conclusion: Fractional counting is more defensible than either ignoring all indirect work or crediting every indirect set as a full direct set.
practical_range_text: Default 0.5 for a clearly loaded secondary mover; use 0-1 when exercise-specific evidence or technique warrants
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Fractional counting is more defensible than either ignoring all indirect work or crediting every indirect set as a full direct set.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How often should a muscle be trained?
conclusion: Frequency is mainly a way to distribute weekly volume and preserve set quality; when volume is equated, hypertrophy differences are small.
practical_range_text: 1-3 sessions per muscle weekly; 2 is a convenient default
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Frequency is mainly a way to distribute weekly volume and preserve set quality; when volume is equated, hypertrophy differences are small.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How many hard sets should be performed per muscle in one session?
conclusion: Spread large weekly volumes across sessions when per-session set quality, repetitions, or target-muscle limitation deteriorates.
key_limitations: No validated universal per-session hypertrophy cap exists.
application_rule: Spread large weekly volumes across sessions when per-session set quality, repetitions, or target-muscle limitation deteriorates.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How long should rest intervals be?
conclusion: Rest long enough to preserve intended repetitions, load, range of motion, and target-muscle performance; compounds usually need longer rests than isolations.
practical_range_text: About 2-5 min for demanding compounds and 1-3 min for stable isolations
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Rest long enough to preserve intended repetitions, load, range of motion, and target-muscle performance; compounds usually need longer rests than isolations.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: What repetition duration supports hypertrophy?
conclusion: Controlled repetitions across a broad range of durations can support hypertrophy; intentionally very slow repetitions are not superior.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Controlled repetitions across a broad range of durations can support hypertrophy; intentionally very slow repetitions are not superior.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Should full range of motion be the default?
conclusion: Use the largest pain-free, controlled range that maintains the intended joint path and target-muscle loading; full ROM is a strong default, not an inviolable rule.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Use the largest pain-free, controlled range that maintains the intended joint path and target-muscle loading; full ROM is a strong default, not an inviolable rule.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Does training at long muscle lengths improve hypertrophy?
conclusion: Including exercises or ranges that load a muscle at relatively long lengths is reasonable, but the average advantage appears small and muscle-specific.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Including exercises or ranges that load a muscle at relatively long lengths is reasonable, but the average advantage appears small and muscle-specific.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Which exercises should be performed first?
conclusion: Place the highest-priority exercise or muscle earlier; hypertrophy appears similar across broad order strategies, while exercise-specific strength favors earlier placement.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Place the highest-priority exercise or muscle earlier; hypertrophy appears similar across broad order strategies, while exercise-specific strength favors earlier placement.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Are machines or free weights superior for hypertrophy?
conclusion: Neither mode is inherently superior for hypertrophy; select for target-muscle loading, comfort, skill, stability, progression resolution, and transfer goals.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Neither mode is inherently superior for hypertrophy; select for target-muscle loading, comfort, skill, stability, progression resolution, and transfer goals.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Does periodization improve hypertrophy?
conclusion: Varying load can support fatigue management and strength, but periodization has not clearly improved hypertrophy when volume is equated.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Varying load can support fatigue management and strength, but periodization has not clearly improved hypertrophy when volume is equated.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: What counts as progression?
conclusion: Progression includes more repetitions or load with comparable execution and effort, the same performance at lower effort, or improved range and control at comparable load.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Progression includes more repetitions or load with comparable execution and effort, the same performance at lower effort, or improved range and control at comparable load.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: When should load increase?
conclusion: Increase load only after the prescribed sets reach the top of their range with target RIR and standardized technique across the exercise-specific confirmation window.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Increase load only after the prescribed sets reach the top of their range with target RIR and standardized technique across the exercise-specific confirmation window.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How much training is needed to maintain muscle?
conclusion: Muscle can often be maintained with substantially less volume than was used to build it if load/effort exposure is retained, but exact trained-male thresholds are unknown.
practical_range_text: Operational default: retain roughly 25-50% of recent productive sets and at least one exposure weekly
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Muscle can often be maintained with substantially less volume than was used to build it if load/effort exposure is retained, but exact trained-male thresholds are unknown.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How much protein supports hypertrophy?
conclusion: Approximately 1.6 g/kg/day covers the average response plateau; up to about 2.2 g/kg/day is a conservative high-end target.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Approximately 1.6 g/kg/day covers the average response plateau; up to about 2.2 g/kg/day is a conservative high-end target.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How much protein supports retention during a cut?
male_population: lean resistance-trained natural males in a deficit
conclusion: Protein needs may rise with leanness and deficit severity; use body weight for ordinary cuts and fat-free-mass scaling for very lean athletes.
practical_range_text: 1.8-2.4 g/kg body weight/day; review-based alternative 2.3-3.1 g/kg FFM/day for lean athletes
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Protein needs may rise with leanness and deficit severity; use body weight for ordinary cuts and fat-free-mass scaling for very lean athletes.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Does protein distribution matter?
conclusion: Total daily protein is primary; distributing it across 3-5 meaningful feedings is a practical secondary consideration.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Total daily protein is primary; distributing it across 3-5 meaningful feedings is a practical secondary consideration.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How large should a deficit be when retaining or gaining muscle matters?
conclusion: Avoid unnecessarily large sustained deficits; around 500 kcal/day was associated with prevented lean-mass gains on average, but retention remains possible with training and protein.
practical_range_text: Often 10-25% below maintenance; use the lower end when leaner or advanced
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Avoid unnecessarily large sustained deficits; around 500 kcal/day was associated with prevented lean-mass gains on average, but retention remains possible with training and protein.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: What rate of loss balances fat loss and retention?
conclusion: Use a slower rate as the trainee becomes leaner, more advanced, or more performance-sensitive.
practical_range_text: About 0.5-1.0%/week for higher-fat or novice males; roughly 0.25-0.75%/week when lean/trained
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Use a slower rate as the trainee becomes leaner, more advanced, or more performance-sensitive.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Who is most likely to recomp?
conclusion: Untrained, detrained, novice, higher-body-fat, and previously under-proteined males have the highest probability; advanced lean males should expect small or absent changes.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Untrained, detrained, novice, higher-body-fat, and previously under-proteined males have the highest probability; advanced lean males should expect small or absent changes.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: How large should a gaining surplus be?
conclusion: Use the smallest surplus that produces a credible upward weight trend and productive training; larger surpluses increasingly add fat rather than guaranteed muscle.
practical_range_text: Often 5-15%; advanced males should generally use the lower end
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Use the smallest surplus that produces a credible upward weight trend and productive training; larger surpluses increasingly add fat rather than guaranteed muscle.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: What rate of weight gain balances muscle and fat?
conclusion: Target gain rate should decline with training age; treat scale change as a control signal, not a direct measure of muscle growth.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Target gain rate should decline with training age; treat scale change as a control signal, not a direct measure of muscle growth.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Should training volume drop during a deficit?
male_population: resistance-trained natural males in a deficit
conclusion: Do not pre-emptively slash volume; retain enough hard work to preserve performance, then reduce low-value sets if recovery or performance deteriorates.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Do not pre-emptively slash volume; retain enough hard work to preserve performance, then reduce low-value sets if recovery or performance deteriorates.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.

research_question: Are advanced set systems superior?
conclusion: Advanced set systems are mainly time-efficiency or variety tools; they are not reliably superior to traditional sets for hypertrophy.
key_limitations: Most syntheses combine sexes and training statuses; exercise- and muscle-specific data are sparse.
application_rule: Advanced set systems are mainly time-efficiency or variety tools; they are not reliably superior to traditional sets for hypertrophy.
exceptions: Pain, technique failure, medical contraindications, or recovery failure override the default.
